title: Decision Compression

content.question: What recurring decision is quietly draining you daily?
sop: List every decision you make more than 3 times a week, what to wear, eat, reply to, or schedule
Turn the top 5 into fixed defaults: a uniform, a meal rotation, a reply template, a standing time slot
Rate your energy each morning and count how many open decisions you're carrying (see calculation, page 5)
Protect one 15-minute block for decisions that actually require judgment, nothing else touches it
Review monthly: kill any default that stopped fitting your life
content.explainer: Every open decision competes for the same limited pool of mental energy, so quality per decision falls as the count of decisions rises, even if you're not physically tired. Compression doesn't mean deciding less; it means deciding once, in advance, for anything that repeats, so the energy you have left goes toward the handful of choices that actually change your outcome.
questions: What recurring decision is quietly draining you daily?
Which decision do you re-make every day that could become a standing default instead?
When your decision quality drops in the afternoon, is it the number of choices piling up, or how tired you already are?
content.equation_example: Energy: 7 out of 10 available today. Load: 14 open decisions on your plate. Quality = 7 divided by 14 = 0.5. Higher (closer to or above 1) means each decision still gets full attention; lower means you're rationing judgment across too many choices and quality slips.
content.closing_statement: Default the repeatable, save judgment for what's not.

caption:
Principle Nº 206: Decision Compression

Limit choices to avoid overwhelm. Every open decision competes for the same limited pool of mental energy, so quality per decision falls as the count of decisions rises, even if you're not physically tired.

Ask yourself: What recurring decision is quietly draining you daily?
